does: This paper proposes Temporally Coherent Reward Modeling (TCRM), a training approach that modifies how reward models in RLHF are regularized during training. Rather than only scoring final response tokens, TCRM adds two regularization terms (corresponding to Monte Carlo and temporal-difference value learning objectives) to the standard Bradley-Terry loss. These regularizers ensure that a reward model's output at any intermediate token position represents the conditional expectation of the final reward given the partial response so far. The method requires no architectural changes, additional data, or inference modifications, yet achieves substantial improvements: token-level accuracy at middle positions rises from 50% to 88.9%, final-token accuracy is preserved, and process reward model performance on ProcessBench reaches 44.9% average F1 using only outcome supervision.
connects: This work builds on the established use of Bradley-Terry models for reward modeling in RLHF systems, extending prior research that recognized reward models as conditional estimators. It connects to classical value function learning in reinforcement learning by establishing that coherent intermediate predictions align with RL principles of value estimation. The paper responds to the recognized limitation that outcome-only reward training wastes signal from intermediate positions in response generation, a gap identified in recent process reward model research. It also relates to broader efforts to improve interpretability and stability of RLHF by deriving predictions from principled probabilistic objectives rather than post-hoc fitting techniques.
enables: By establishing that well-trained reward models function as value functions, this work enables several downstream capabilities. Intermediate reward scores become interpretable and reliable for training process reward models without requiring expensive process-level annotations, reducing data requirements for improving step-level feedback. The coherent token trajectories unlock better credit assignment in iterative RLHF and enable more principled curriculum learning strategies. The theoretical connection to value learning opens paths for applying RL optimization techniques directly to reward modeling, potentially improving sample efficiency. The approach suggests that reward models can serve dual purposes as both outcome evaluators and step-level feedback providers, simplifying multi-stage alignment pipelines and enabling stronger supervision signals for long-horizon language model training.
